speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six, Paul Goldstein. Tell me about post-war and how your life went.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well, because of the period that we just covered, we-- I can't say that I had a very normal, in the sense of everybody else's kind of childhood. Because from the moment the Germans occupied a country, we were already refugees. We were already segregated. And then we had this horrible two years of what I would call a private concentration camp. And then we had our financial difficulties after the war. And I must say that my-- the same father, who I admired for his courage and for his-- and for his behavior during the war in terms of the surviving, was at that very same age, was also a very, very autocratic person. Very authoritarian. So I had a very rough time with that as well after the war. Which had nothing to do with being Jewish, it just happens to be in my family. And my-- but I always, always had a very, very strong desire to develop my mind and-- and to get an education to better myself. I always had that, from-- from very early on. So-- so after the community reassembled whatever was left, the Tachkemoni School was reopened. And it had a makeshift grade [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seven for people who, by age, would go into that grade. And then all kinds of different levels of education depending on where they came from. Some had been in schools in-- in other countries, or-- or in Switzerland or whatever, throughout the war. So they were OK. Others had to make up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was thrown in such a class and to try and get as up to date as possible with some crash courses, so that I could, at my normal age, entered the local high school. I was admitted to high school. It was a six-year program. There were-- the failure rate was enormous because there was massive unemployment and they weren't interested in graduating too many people. So I would say that the failure rate there was, over the six years, about 95%. So my dad said, as long-- this was a state school, there was no cost involved-- said, as long as you can pass your grades, you can keep going. So I managed to finish that.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of that, however, that high school leaving diploma, senior matric-- and-- and it was only good for continuing education, because it didn't give you a profession or a trade. And my desire was to go into journalism.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the logical place to go was to go to the Free University in Brussels. So I sent in my-- my-- my record, and they said, fine. I'm accepted. But the tuition fees were pretty steep, and we didn't have any money. So this was the beginning of my frustrations with further education. So I could not continue. So I couldn't continue. Then I was drafted in the armed forces because it was compulsory military service. So I served two years in the Belgium Army. So after high school in the Belgian armed forces, I was, at that point in time, I had a high school education, which wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't have any skills to go into any kind of work. There was massive unemployment. It was very hard to get a job. I didn't have the money. Money was always a problem in our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after I got-- I found a job with a-- with a-- with a bank, a kind of position that wasn't very enticing, but it was better than nothing. And I saw there was no future. I decided I had to get out. If I ever wanted to get an education in-- in-- a better education, a better field, to get out of the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know where to go or where to turn. I was not very wise in the ways of the world. And I was-- I-- I-- I-- I was quite intelligent. But I did not have any maturity, because the way I was raised, and brought up, and held back, I had the mentality-- when I was 21 years old, I probably had the-- the maturity of-- of-- of-- of a 12-year-old. Wasn't very good equipment. But I had a certain drive, and a certain self-confidence, and a certain desire. So the question was to get out. So through proce-- process of elimination, Canada became the country of destination of choice. The United States I could have gone to, but as soon as I got there, they would have sent me to Korea in the war, even though I had already served two years in a NATO Army. The Belgian Congo was not an option, because I didn't want to be lording it over natives. Plus, many of the youngsters that went there came back with tropical diseases.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had been liberated by Canada-- by Canadians. I had spoken to French Canadians. I'd asked them what kind of soldiers, and they told me about the newspaper in Montreal. You could speak French. And since I had never spoken English till then, and Frenc-- I was pretty fluent in French, I felt that there was a possibility going to Montreal would solve all my problems. Now, Belgium-- to get out of Belgium was very easy, because Belgians, by and large, don't-- don't emigrate. The Dutch-- the Dutch had a phenomenal amount of emigrants. So did the Italians, and the Germans, and others, but not Belgians. So the Germans had a long waiting list because all were above their quotas. Holland even had an emigration ministry to help finance people's emigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me you arrived in Canada with $200?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. $200 and-- and a suitcase with whatever pocket books I had been able to put together. And that was my start in this country. I was told by the attache in the-- of the Canadian embassy in Brussels that Canada was a land of golden opportunity. And people like myself, we had it made here based on whatever I told them about myself. He didn't tell me that there was the height of unemployment. But he had a quota to fill, and there was almost-- he was happy to be able to send somebody over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came here. I didn't know anybody. I didn't have a trade. I didn't have anything. I was told by immigration to wait. They called me-- there's a job opening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And lo and behold, to make a long story short, because the arrival in the train, and the plane-- and the ship, I should say-- so there were many interesting stories, but they're not-- they're not to the point. The point was that I came desperate to succeed and to make something of-- of myself. And my first job after two weeks was assistantship in a dry goods store in Montreal, which-- which was a glorified title for a floor sweeper. I hated that job. I tried to get out of it. I went to the Jewish vocational service. He said, be happy have that job. There's lots of unemployment. I'm talking about the end of '54. I-- I took a room and board in a Romanian family, $60 a month, and sent the other $140 home to-- back to my parents. So I didn't have any money. At the end of the month, I had $12 left. And I-- I couldn't stand the work because it was dirty. I can tell episodes that was really-- I felt it was corrupt. And so I came to Canada, not go into details, just to save some time here. And then I put an ad in the-- a $6 ad in the as well as in saying that, equivalent to two years of college-- multilingual-- French, English, and so forth-- seeks an opportunity. And I got a call from a travel agent who needed someone who spoke French well, because they were dealing with the-- with the clergy and the missionaries and arranging their papers and going around to all the consulates. It was Madek's Travel Agency. Mr. Madek, a Jewish travel agent. And I got that job. The pay wasn't much better, but at least at an office, I had a chance to improve myself. So I spent a lot of time with monsignors talking theology and sitting in waiting rooms of these-- of all these consulates. Unfortunately, Mr. Madek, in that-- in that spring, during that spring, sent his family while out in Israel on a visit-- his wife and two children. And they were on the plane that's straight over the Bulgarian border. And they were shot down by the Bulgarian Air Force and killed. It was very traumatic. It changed him completely. I didn't have a future there anymore. So I-- I-- I-- I responded to an ad by the [INAUDIBLE]. They needed someone at the information desk that spoke languages. Again, the languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got that job. I went to the Jewish vocational service. I says, I want to go to school. What opportunities are there? They said, if you're a hard-working person, you're a good student, then there's an institution called St. George Williams University or College, which is now Concordia. And there you can work-- you can be a working person and still continue an education.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and your pace is your own. You can take three credits during the-- our-- our average daytime student takes five credits a year. On a  part-time basis, you can take three credits in the winter and two in the summer. That's the same five credits if you can handle it. But first, you have to go there and be tested. So I went there with my diploma from high school. I had some tests. The tests came out exceptionally well. As a matter of fact, they told me that I-- that I was doctorate material. So this was very encouraging.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went-- so I worked and went, and then-- then the-- then there was an opportunity to get hired by a big packaging firm as the cost accounting department. So I worked with this packaging firm. And during the day, I-- I was at the-- at the Y. I went into some very advanced physical fitness to recover that. And I also went to school during the daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did graduate. I got my BA with honors, sufficiently to be able to be accepted at the master's program at McGill, at Carleton, where I went into political philosophy. And economics was my main subject. Unfortunately, my employer kept moving me around because I became sales manager for the firm, so I could never finish. But after 10 years, I reached the top of that company-- top level-- and I was earmarked to become the general manager. Unfortunately, or, I-- at the same time, I had become very involved in the issue of-- of fighting hate propaganda. This was always a subject close to my heart. And in 1960 or '61, at the-- capture of Eichmann signaled the-- the revival of Nazi movements around the world. And a lot of people that had gone through the experience couldn't believe that they saw swastikas again, and Nazis holding meetings. The [INAUDIBLE] in Toronto and the [INAUDIBLE] in Montreal. Talking about the early '60s. And they desperately needed someone who could communicate with the French press. And there again, my French came-- came in-- came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got involved with that. And I got so deeply involved, that I spent 10 years of my life. I made it my top priority, on a full-time basis, to fight this scourge at every possible level. Nothing was more important to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: All my emotions found an outlet into-- into doing that very effectively. Unfortunately, at the expense of my own self-interest, at the expense of starting a family, at the expense of looking after myself financially.

speaker: interviewer
text: But those things came later. You started a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I finally reached a point where I-- I reached a point that I saw that, if I continued that way, that I-- that I would never amount to anything. And I had to start changing my priorities. Start a family and look after myself. So-- and that's what I did. And a colleague in the Association of Survivors of Nazi Oppression, of which I eventually became the president, at the time suggested I would be very cut out for a life insurance career. I said, very  sorry. It's not for me. My experiences with life insurance agents hasn't been that favorable. He said, look, you don't know what levels there are. Come and see-- see the manager. I went in there in-- in December of 1966. They were-- was a New York Life Insurance Company in Montreal. I was told-- a law book was pulled out to show a wonderful career you could have.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't understand what they were talking about. But I  said, how much money can you make? Because I was desperate. And-- because, you see, because of all my activities, I had- I had-- I had to choose between pursuing full-time fighting hate propaganda and Nazis, or take on the general managership of my packaging firm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't handle both. And I went with my-- with my heart instead of with my pocket. And I abandoned the job and the other thing didn't-- I couldn't make a living at the other-- on the other side. So I really fell flat and I had to start all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't want to become a drifter, so it took me a long-- many months before I decided on what career I would follow. But when the manager told me that-- showed me what some of the other people were making, I says, well, where do I sign? Because my self-confidence told me that, whatever anybody else could do, I could also do. I started in the wrong way, doing the wrong thing, at the wrong time, but eventually developed. And I'm now in my 30th year. And  I'm now considered quite an-- quite the-- what's the word? Professional financial planner. And I do work with accounting firms and law firms in the more advanced estate planning areas. And I also have a brokerage firm for-- for retirement planning and life insurance. And-- And you've been very successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've reached a-- a good level of success, because I stoo-- I stayed with it and did it. I couldn't change my-- I didn't want to adjust to the-- to the-- to the standard of industry. So I adjusted my [INAUDIBLE] to my personality, did everything in a professional, took every possible course, and got myself educated in-- at handling this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your drive to improve yourself and to succeed has something to do with what you've been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. First of all, it's a combination of what I went through. But then it's also my-- my-- my personality that I inherited, probably, from my parents, who were both fighters and survivors. But I can say one thing, that when you-- when you-- when-- when-- when you live in total fear, and-- and-- and-- and with hunger, and thirst pains for years, and-- and-- and-- and where a broken egg almost gets you killed, and where you live from-- and-- and a piece of chocolate is like an impossible dream, and you find-- and given the opportunity to-- to-- to-- to-- to develop your-- your-- your personal talents, and, on merit, to improve your lifestyle, I think it's a phenomenal motivation that, no matter what the obstacles, and no matter what the setbacks, to-- to-- it gives you a drive to make the very best of-- of-- of-- of the short life span that you have, and-- and-- and-- and-- and to raise your standards to-- to-- to-- to the fullest possible potential of whatever-- whatever God-given talents you have. Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for giving us this interview. We'll take a short break.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Naomi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are married- we are married almost 29 years now. We met in 1967-- the-- yeah, met in 1967 in Montreal. I-- I just reached a point where I had decided I was going to look after myself and start a family life. So the word spread around that I was eligible. Naomi just arrived in Canada a few months before. She was a widow with two small children, lost her husband, and she came to be with her mother who lived here, and her brother who lived here, in Toronto. Mutual friends thought that we were a perfect match. I didn't think so at first, because I wasn't interested in starting with already ready family. But they told me that, if I would overcome that reluctance and give a chance to meet, that there would be redeeming qualities. And I should find out for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after many trials and errors, we finally met on May 19th, 1967. And six weeks later, we were married. And we have our own additional child-- our own son, who we will talk about in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must say that, after all these 29 years, if I had a chance to do it over again, I wouldn't hesitate one second, because she's the best thing that ever happened to me in my life. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Naomi, maybe  you could tell about the children, their names, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the children are in or-- chronological order-- Ruth, who is in Israel. And she has three children, three girls. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the next is Micah, who is also in Israel and is married, but no children. And the third one is David, who is here, and being-- being the youngest, but orthodox, has already four children. So that's-- that's the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all together, how many grandchildren have you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven. Seven, yes, and growing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the identity card that my mother was carrying at the time that we-- the persecution started to go much stronger. I'm talking about 1942. It shows a date it was issued on the inside of May, 1942. And it also showed how the Jewish people were segregated and identified. For instance, you see at the top it says, Belgium, the Belgium-- Kingdom of Belgium, 'Koninkrijk Belgie.' Now, because they were born outside of Belgium, right away, there is this big red line that says, foreigner, even though they-- they lived there and were actually citizens. But from the German point of view, they were foreigners. It also shows in the stamp under the-- under the heading, Belgium, it shows a red stamp with a number. And it says there-- it says that-- OK. It says, 'registered in the Jews register,' under that number. That's my mother's number in the-- in the Jewish registry. Then on the inside, if we turn it around--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll look at the inside now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yes, on the inside, you'll see in the top left corner is the stamp of the Shield of David, the Magen David, my mother's maiden name, Tiger. Tiger. And then there is a stamp, 'Jood Juif,' identifying her as being Jewish. That is the identity card. It also shows the date of issue. It shows the date of issue in the middle, in red, where it says, '11 May,' May in  Dutch, '1942.' So this was the-- my mother's-- this was the identity card of a-- a-- a Jewish person in Belgium, adult, in-- in-- in that particular episode. That's how the Jews were identified, and isolated, and registered before the extermination. This is the only picture I have, and which I somehow found in my mother's possessions, of my paternal grandparents, who I never recall meeting. That's-- those are the people that we probably visited when I was one year old, once, but that my father later on helped out with a home. But from all the accounts I heard, the Lemberg and Zamarstynow were places where there were 100% death rates. There were no survivors whatsoever. There were atrocious stories. All these people perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, my-- my grandfather-- my-- as I said, my grandfather-- you know, when you talk about that, it's-- local people here in Canada are-- are-- don't-- don't understand the psychology of people that don't grow up with-- with-- with grandparents, never knew their grandparents. My-- my grandchildren are very lucky that they have their grandparents around them. Anyway, so this is my ancestor, Yakob Schulum Levi Goldstein and his wife, Frima Goldstein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a picture of Rene, my younger brother, and myself, taken just around the time that we were separated from my mother and went into hiding with the Roggemans in Hove, Belgium. OK. This picture on the left was taken in front of the house where we were in hiding during World War II, 12 [INAUDIBLE] in Hove, near Antwerp. And the local shepherds had just passed through with these two sheep, and he was good enough to have us take a picture with the two sheep, which was quite an experience for us city kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The picture on the right is we are standing in front of the house where we were hidden. Mind you, these pictures were all taken at the beginning. I-- the way we looked, a couple of years later, we would have been unrecognizable. We had a haggard and scared look, and gaunt look, very much like people that you see come out of-- of concentration camps. These are my late father and mother, two very, very courageous people without-- with-- with-- without them, I wouldn't be here talking about them. This picture was taken the first time I went back to Belgium for a visit. This was taken, I believe, around 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remind me their full names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's Joseph Goldstein, my late father, and Nesie Goldstein, my mother, who-- he-- Joseph would escape from a train to Auschwitz. And-- and Nesie Goldstein would spend two years in a camp in Mechelen, in Belgium, was one of the handful of people who survived out of the 28,000 that were sent in that transport. And she had the foresight and-- and-- to-- to-- to separate herself from her children, and, as a result, saved our lives as well. And she managed to-- to live and hang in there till a year ago. She passed away a year ago in April, at the senior home in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fortunately, eight months before, I felt, when I saw her health going down, I-- and she had never seen the latest great grandchild. So I took David and the children. Remember the episode where I talked about the hotel where they stayed? Yes. I think we'll see a picture of your mother with the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, which is the last picture of my mother alive with the children. And she was so happy. That's how I always want to remember her. Yes, the smiling lady there is my mother, the last time she saw her great grandchildren. The one she holds in her lap is-- is Nacham. The middle one is Avraham Dov. And the little doll on the right is my princess, Sarah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the latest shot of the four-- of our four grandchildren in Canada. The-- the little face on the left is Nacham, then Sarah in the back, Avraham Dov on the right, and then the new arrival, Natan, was born three months ago in the middle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here we have a picture of little Nacham and my son, David, who I mentioned is ultraorthodox. And this is Motzei Shabbat when he's in his white kittel, and his shtriemel, and probably representing his-- showing off, I would say-- his very strict observance.
